Maya: Welcome back to Singletrack Sessions. Today we're talking about drivetrain mechanics, and I'm joined by Noah Bryer, mechanic at Spoke & Wrench. Noah Bryer, thanks for being here.
Noah Bryer: Thanks, Maya. Great to be back.
Maya: Today's episode is sponsored by Notion. Notion replaces the dozen tools your team is half-using. Try notion.com today.Before we get into it — Sophie Lorent was on last episode talking about tire pressure mattering more than suspension tweaks.
Noah Bryer: Yeah, exactly. And it ties into what we're covering today.
Maya: I want to flag something — Sophie Lorent mentioned this same dynamic a couple episodes back.I want to flag something — Liam Vandermeer mentioned this same dynamic a couple episodes back.I want to dig into that. What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Noah Bryer: It comes down to this: Most mystery creaks are solved by cleaning contact surfaces and re-torquing to spec. We've seen this play out at teams using Spoke & Wrench. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.
Maya: That's a useful framing. Where do maintenance and drivetrain mechanics intersect?
Noah Bryer: Here's how I think about it. A bike should feel quiet and predictable so your attention stays on the trail. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Maya: I want to dig into that. What's the structural angle most listeners don't have access to?
Noah Bryer: Sure. Chain wear is the single best leading indicator of how soon you'll be replacing cassettes. We've seen this play out at teams using Spoke & Wrench. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.
Maya: Say more about that.
Noah Bryer: Spoke & Wrench is one of the platforms where this shows up cleanly. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams.
Maya: Take me through the decision. How does RockShox fit into this picture?
Noah Bryer: Sure. Suspension service intervals are a risk-management decision. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.

Noah Bryer: Sure. Most mystery creaks are solved by cleaning contact surfaces and re-torquing to spec. We've seen this play out at teams using Spoke & Wrench. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams.

Noah Bryer: And when teams adopt Shimano in that flow, the trade-off becomes visible — I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams.

Noah Bryer: Pick one of the ideas we covered and run it for two weeks. The rest is iteration.
